stations.label: THE COINAGE ACT BIRTH
stations.line: They passed the Coinage Act and put me on paper backed by silver and gold, and I started with nothing but a promise and the nerve to make people believe it.
stations.era_photo: A young man in a plain dark wool frock coat and high linen stock, standing in the newly built Philadelphia Mint, rendered as a hand-tinted engraving on rag paper, the only reproduction process of 1792.

stations.label: THE GREENBACK GOES TO WAR
stations.line: The Union needed money to fight and there was no gold to spare, so I printed the greenback and asked a bleeding country to trust ink over metal — and it did.
stations.era_photo: A stern man in a Civil War-era black suit and wide bow tie, seated beside stacked bond ledgers in a Washington Treasury office, captured as a sepia-toned albumen print with the stiff long-exposure stillness of 1861.

stations.label: THE FEDERAL RESERVE STANDS UP
stations.line: They built me a central bank and a lender of last resort, and for the first time I had a floor under my feet instead of just my own word.
stations.era_photo: A confident man in a three-piece pinstripe suit and pocket watch chain, standing on the steps of a granite Federal Reserve building, shot as a crisp black-and-white silver gelatin photograph typical of 1913.

stations.label: BRETTON WOODS CROWNS ME
stations.line: Forty-four nations sat in a New Hampshire hotel and pinned the whole world's exchange to me at thirty-five dollars an ounce — I walked in a currency and walked out the system.
stations.era_photo: A commanding man in a broad-shouldered double-breasted suit at the Mount Washington Hotel conference table, delegates behind him, rendered as a grainy wartime black-and-white silver gelatin press photograph, July 1944.

stations.label: THE GOLD WINDOW CLOSES
stations.line: The reserves were draining and there was no floor left to defend, so on a Sunday night I told a hundred and fifty million people I was cutting the cord to gold — and I did it before I told anyone I should have.
stations.era_photo: A resolute man in a wide-lapel 1970s suit and thick knotted tie, addressing a television camera under hot studio lights, captured on warm-toned Kodachrome color film with the slight softness of 1971 broadcast footage.

stations.label: THE FLOOR HOLDS ANYWAY
stations.line: The whole system I anchor nearly went down, and yet when the panic hit, the world ran to me — they hated the crisis I sat at the center of and they still bought me by the trillion.
stations.era_photo: A tense man in a modern dark suit, no tie, sleeves at the wrist, on a trading floor lit by red screens, shot as an early high-ISO digital DSLR photograph with the flat news-wire clarity of 2008.

stations.label: THE FLOOR THAT DECLINES
stations.line: I'm still the reserve the world reaches for in every crisis, and I know the momentum isn't all mine anymore — I don't say that in public, because saying it makes it true faster.
stations.era_photo: A composed older man in a tailored charcoal suit, standing before a wall of live market data, rendered as a sharp full-frame digital photograph with the cool color grading and shallow depth of field of 2025.
